title: The Working Capital Credit Multiplier
abstract: We provide novel evidence that funding frictions can limit firms’ short-term investments in receivables and inventories, reducing their production capacity. We propose a credit multiplier driven by these considerations and empirically isolate its importance by comparing how a similar firm responds to shocks differently when these shocks are initiated in their most profitable quarter (“main quarter”). We implement this test using recurring and unpredictable shocks (e.g., oil shocks) and provide extensive evidence supporting our identification strategy. Our results suggest that funding constraints and credit multiplier effects are significant for smaller firms that heavily rely on financing from suppliers.

article.introduction:
One of the central questionsin finance is how financing frictions affect firms and the real economy. An influential idea in this context is the existence of a financial accelerator or credit multiplier effect (e.g., Bernanke and Gertler (1989) and Kiyotaki and Moore (1997)). In the presence of financing frictions, short-term operating losses can lead to borrowing problems that are both amplified and propagated over time through their impact on firms’ balance sheets. This effect can play an important role in explaining the causes and consequences of funding problems that firms face during recessions, industry downturns, or episodes of financial distress.
In this paper, we propose and analyze a new mechanism through which the credit multiplier can affect firms’ operations and economic activity—the “working capital credit multiplier.” The key idea behind this multiplier is that funding frictions can also impact short-term investments in assets such as accounts receivables and inventories. Existing literature focuses largely on the effect of financing frictions on longer-term investment.1The focus on long-term investment is reasonable in principle because short-term assets may be easier to finance. However, financing frictions that affect short-term investments can also have important implications because these investments immediately affect firms’ operations: lower inventories or receivables translate into lower sales. Consider, for example, a retailer that needs to buy inventories prior to sales or a construction company that needs to perform its work prior to billing clients. As firms need to cover operating costs prior to the collection of cash from sales, there is a need for short-term financing of their production. Constraints on firms’ ability to fund these short-term investments can limit their production capacity and directly influence firms’ sales and economic activity.2This point has been emphasized by a theoretical macrofinance literature.3As far as we know, however, there is no microlevel empirical evidence on whether financing frictions impact short-term investments and production capacity.4Our main goal in this paper is to provide such evidence as well as to document a new credit multiplier effect driven by these specific considerations.
We start our analysis by outlining the mechanics of the credit multiplier effect and its predictions. When firms need to cover operating costs prior to the collection of cash from sales, it is natural for firms to use short-term credit. We label this short-term funding as working capital financing and consider firms that rely largely on suppliers (payables) to cover this financing need. As customers purchase inputs from suppliers, a share of these inputs is sold by suppliers on credit, which is repaid after the collection of cash from sales by customers. The idea that suppliers provide this type of short-term funding to smaller firms is supported by both anecdotal evidence and empirical research.5We build on this literature by analyzing a constrained firm that uses all of the credit available from suppliers and faces funding frictions that limit the availability of this credit.
The working capital credit multiplier captures the implications of these financing frictions for the amplification and propagation of shocks over time. Since a share of a firm's new inputs are financed by suppliers, the responses of the firm's short-term investments to economic shocks are levered and hence amplified. For example, suppose a firm can finance 30% of its inputs on credit and funds the remaining 70% of its inputs using internal funds. If the firm initially reduces its inputs by 1.0 unit in response to a shock, the leverage from suppliers will lead to a total decrease in inputs of (1.0)/ (0.7) = 1.43 units. Moreover, this levered response of short-term investments to shocks is propagated over time. As firms cut receivables and inventories, their production capacity and sales are reduced, which decreases their net worth in subsequent periods. Lower internal funds in subsequent periods lead in turn to further reductions in firms’ short-term investments and sales in the future, propagating the effects of funding constraints over time.
A central feature of our analysis is the idea that these effects interact with seasonal patterns in firm profitability. As we document in SectionIII.B, there are strong seasonal patterns in the cash flows of a broad range of U.S. firms (Chang et al. (2017)). Firms have main periods (quarters), in which their cash flows are predicted to be significantly stronger. We show how the effects described above should be stronger when shocks hit during a firm's main quarter. The intuition is that firms should be able to finance a larger share of inputs on credit during their main quarter, when they are more profitable and have more cash flows to repay suppliers.6This increased leverage in the main quarter amplifies the responses of firms’ sales and short-term investments to shocks (in percentage terms).7
If propagation effects are sufficiently strong, our framework leads to the further implication that shocks that hit firms inside their main quarter can have stronger percentage effects on short-term investment and production (sales) not only contemporaneously but also in subsequent periods. To see this, suppose a shock hits in quartert, and in quartertfirmiis in its main period while a similar firmjis outside of its main period. As we move forward in time, firmjwill eventually move into its main period, say quartert+ 1. If the shock is persistent in nature, then the direct effect of the shock in quartert+ 1 should be stronger for firmjthan for firmi, as firmjis in its main period whereas firmiis not. However, the stronger initial effect of the shock for firmiin quartertwill translate into stronger propagation effects in quartert+ 1. Hence, propagation effects sufficiently large can lead to stronger responses by firmiin both quartertand quartert+ 1.
In our empirical analysis, we compare how similar firms’ responses to shocks differ based on whether the shocks hit in their main quarter. We label this contrast as the main quarter effect and focus on this effect for two reasons. First, these seasonal interactions illustrate how initial conditions can shape credit multiplier effects. Second, the analysis of the main quarter effects helps us empirically isolate the effects of the working capital multiplier.
Our empirical approach to isolate the working capital credit multiplier is to examine whether significant main quarter effects are present in firms’ immediate as well as subsequent responses to shocks. While the presence of a main quarter effect in firms’ immediate responses to shocks is consistent with the multiplier we analyze, this pattern could be driven by other factors. For instance, even without frictions in working capital financing, firms might have stronger immediate responses to economic shocks such as input price increases during their main quarter. However, main quarter effects in both immediate and subsequent quarters must capture a strongerpropagationover time for shocks that hit in the main quarter. The intuition for this point is as follows. If firmj(the control firm above) is outside its main quarter when the shock is initiated (say quartert), it eventually will move into its main quarter in a subsequent period (say quartert+ 1). Hence, a stronger response to the shock by firmi(the treated firm) in quartertimplies that the direct effect of the shock should be stronger for firmj(the control firm) in quartert+ 1. But suppose we find that firmialso shows a greater response to the shock in quartert+ 1, relative to firmj. Such a result must indicate a propagation of the shock over time.
Such a propagation effect is consistent with the credit multiplier, as changes in net worth propagate the effect of the shock over time. However, in principle, this propagation effect could be driven by alternative mechanisms. For example, consider an input price shock that has a stronger immediate effect on firms’ sales in their main quarter. If the firm fires some workers as its sales decline, the initial effect of the shock on employment could drive its propagation over time. To address this concern, we examine whether propagation effects concentrate among firms that rely strongly on credit from their suppliers. We also refine our approach to incorporate additional contrasts between effects for short- versus longer-term investments, as well as contrasts between different types of firms and shocks. These refinements allow us to further address potential concerns with our empirical approach.
An important component of our analysis is the comparison of similar firms hit by a shock inside versus outside their main quarter. As we discuss in SectionII, we operationalize this test by analyzing recurring shocks that are unpredictable, that is, that have a constant probability over time that cannot be predicted using past information. Our approach predicts that such shocks lead to a random assignment of treated firms (hit inside the main quarter) and control firms (hit outside the main quarter). We provide direct evidence that the treated and control firms in our analysis are very similar along many basic characteristics and that controlling for potential differences across them has virtually no effect on our results.
The credit multiplier that we want to isolate should be particularly relevant for shocks that have a stronger effect on firms’ short-term investments in the absence of leverage from suppliers. In particular, our theoretical framework predicts that shocks to input prices (production costs) should matter the most.8Motivated by these considerations, in our main analysis we construct a broad sample of industry shocks using industries’ negative exposures to oil shocks. These shocks are largely unpredictable, recurring, and associated with significant changes in production costs. We analyze the differential effect of these industry shocks on firms that happen to be inside their main quarter when the shock hits and we consider alternative recurring shocks when implementing our analysis.
Using this approach, we detect significant propagation effects for smaller firms that rely strongly on credit from suppliers but not for other smaller firms. Shocks that hit these firms in their main quarter have significantly stronger effects on their sales and short-term investments (receivables and inventories) in both the first and second semesters after the start of the shock. Moreover, these patterns are differentially stronger for firms that rely strongly on credit from suppliers relative to other smaller firms, and this contrast across the responses of different types of firms remains stable as we control for differences in basic firm characteristics.
We then compare these effects for short- and long-term investments (capital expenditures). In principle, propagation effects could be equally or more important for long-term investments. As the initial effects of the shock reduce a firm's net worth, the firm has less internal funds to finance its investments. However, our framework can also generate an asymmetric effect on short-term investment. To show this point, we build on previous work analyzing the implications of financing frictions (Bernanke, Gertler, and Gilchrist (1999)), and assume that drops in firms’ internal resources adversely affect new borrowing terms.9When firms finance a share of their short-term investments with credit from suppliers, reductions in firms’ internal funds will immediately increase the marginal cost of expanding these investments. Intuitively, these short-term investments are exposed to changes in refinancing terms for this short-term credit. Since long-term investments are more likely to be financed using long-term term financing (Petersen and Rajan (1997)), these investments are less exposed to this refinancing effect (see SectionIV.C). This asymmetry in the exposure to refinancing terms induces a substitution across these two types of investment, which amplifies propagation effects for short-term investments and mitigates these effects for long-term investments.
Consistent with this idea, we find that the previous effects are stronger for firms’ short-term investment relative to their long-term investment. The main quarter effects for firms’ immediate and subsequent responses to shocks are stronger for short-term investments (payables and are receivables). Moreover, also consistent with the intuition above, we do not find economically or statistically significant propagation effects for long-term investment.
As described above, the main quarter effects that we analyze build on the idea that customers can finance a larger share of inputs on credit during their main quarter and they are constrained, borrowing all the credit available from suppliers. If this condition is satisfied in the data, firms’ payables should increase relative to their short-term investment (inventories and receivables) during their main quarter, that is, their leverage from suppliers should increase in the main quarter. This leverage seasonality is a necessary condition for the mechanism we propose to be empirically relevant. Based on this insight, we explore which firms could be potentially exposed to multiplier effects. First, further supporting our focus on smaller firms that rely strongly on credit from suppliers, we show that leverage seasonality is present for these firms but is not present for other smaller firms.10Second, we confirm that there is no leverage seasonality for firms that rely strongly on short-term debt rather than credit from suppliers. Thus, we should not detect main quarter effects for these firms, which is precisely what we find. We also find that main quarter effects are stronger for firms that rely on credit from suppliers, compared to firms that rely on short-term debt. Third, we document that leverage seasonality is not present for firms that are unlikely to face significant liquidity issues—large firms, high-cash firms, and high-payout firms. We then show that the main quarter effects that we document are not present for any of these firms.
We additionally examine the previous effects using a broader range of economic shocks. As discussed above, our mechanism predicts that these effects should be most relevant for shocks strongly associated with changes in production costs. Our results support this prediction.
In our final analysis (SectionVI), we examine potential alternative explanations for our results. We explain that our approach to isolate propagation effects does not depend on assumptions about factors other than whether a firm is hit by a shock when it is inside (treated) versus outside (control) their main quarter, such as the persistence of shocks. Our empirical approach is designed to ensure that treated and control firms are otherwise similar, and we provide multiple sets of evidence supporting this idea.
We interpret the documented propagation effects as evidence of a working capital credit multiplier. However, in principle, alternative mechanisms could also rationalize the propagation effects that we uncover even when treated and control firms are similar. For example, stronger initial effects on sales might lead a firm to lose some customers who do not switch back to the firm in future periods, translating into a propagation of initial conditions over time. Our evidence is not consistent with these alternative explanations. For example, we find that these propagation effects are stronger for firms’ short-term investments and are not associated with significant changes in long-term investment. If firms persistently face reduced demand by customers, their return on long-term investments should decline. Thus, under this alternative explanation we should see reductions in long-term investment (we do not). In addition, the propagation effects that we document are only significant and important for firms that rely strongly on credit from suppliers (which as we show are exposed to our mechanism). It is unclear why alternative explanations would lead to propagation effects only in this specific subset of firms, but not in other firms. Also consistent with our mechanism, we detect significant propagation effects only for shocks more strongly associated with changes in production costs.11While it is difficult to completely rule out alternative interpretations for the propagation effects that we document, taken together our evidence is most consistent with the working capital credit multiplier.
Overall, our main contribution is to provide microlevel evidence that an important subset of firms faces funding frictions that limit their ability to finance short-term investments in payables and receivables and that reduce their production capacity. This analysis of short-term investment considerations, and a credit multiplier driven by such effects, distinguishes our paper from the literature that focuses on the impact of financing frictions on long-term investments.12Our paper is also related to Amiti and Weinstein (2011) and Paravisini et al. (2015), who provide evidence that working capital financing constraints are relevant for exporting activity during financial crises in Japan and Peru, respectively. In addition, our focus on supplier financing connects our work with the literature on trade credit as well as previous evidence on contagion effects from financially constrained suppliers (Costello (2020)).13Distinct from previous research, we illustrate the role of supplier financing in limiting the short-term investments and production capacity of customers. Our evidence on the propagation of funding problems over time is also a relevant contribution to the literature. While this propagation mechanism is an important feature of theories of the credit multiplier (e.g., Kiyotaki and Moore (1997)), there is little empirical evidence of this propagation. Our analysis of a within-firm propagation mechanism and the role of short-term investments in this process complements the evidence of Huber (2018) on the propagation of funding problems over time at the regional level in Germany.
The paper is organized as follows. SectionIlays out our theoretical framework. SectionIIexplains our empirical strategy. SectionIIIdescribes the data that we use in our analysis and documents several patterns that support our empirical strategy. SectionIVpresents our main results. SectionVpresents a few auxiliary results and SectionVIdiscusses alternative interpretations. We conclude the paper in SectionVII.
article.I. Theoretical Framework:
We begin by discussing the theoretical framework that we use to motivate our empirical tests. We focus on the main intuitions here; we provide a simple model formalizing them in theAppendix. The starting point for our analysis is the notion that firms’ operations require short-term investments in accounts receivable and inventories, which creates a need for working capital financing. In each period (e.g., quarter), firms need to cover operating costs prior to the collection of cash from sales and thus they need working capital financing to fund this gap. Two examples are a retailer that needs funding to buy goods prior to their sale to final customers, or a construction company that needs funding to make progress on construction projects prior to billing their clients. We label this investment in input costs in each period as the firm's short-term investment. In our empirical analysis, we interpret this investment as capturing short-term investments in inventories and receivables.
We consider a firm that relies largely on funding from suppliers (accounts payable) to cover this financing need. Suppliers provide short-term credit at the beginning of each period and get paid at the end of the period when the firm generates cash from sales. The idea that suppliers provide this type of short-term funding to smaller firms has been highlighted by previous research and is supported by both anecdotal and empirical evidence.14We refer to this short-term funding as working capital financing.
We examine a constrained firm that would like to expand its short-term investment using additional working capital financing but faces frictions. The firm uses all of the credit available from suppliers, but frictions limit the amount of credit that is available. Because firms rely on short-term investments for the completion of their sales, binding constraints on the funding of these investments translate into immediate reductions in sales, reducing the firm's production capacity. We examine the implications of these financing frictions for the amplification and propagation of shocks over time. We refer to these effects as the working capital credit multiplier.
The predictions that we analyze build on a key point that is consistent with both previous research and anecdotal evidence. The share of inputs that customers can finance on credit from suppliers is not determined entirely by the value of the goods being sold, but it is also shaped by customers’ cash flows. For example, Petersen and Rajan (1997) document that more profitable customers receive significantly more credit from suppliers. Lian and Ma (2021) provide evidence on the importance of firms’ income, as opposed to hard assets, in shaping corporate lending terms in the United States.15
This insight motivates the two main assumptions that we rely on. First, as firms buy goods on credit, a share of the new inputs that they use is financed by suppliers. Consider a firm that buys an additional unit of input from suppliers. This investment in inputs allows the firm to raise financing that is backed by the increase in cash flows. To give a numerical example, suppose that the firm can finance 30% of inputs on credit. Then, for every 0.7 units of inputs financed with internal funds, 0.3 additional units can be purchased on credit from suppliers.
When the firm is constrained and uses all the available credit, this financing leads to a levered response of the firm's short-term investment to economic shocks. For example, suppose the firm described above experiences an increase in input prices. As the constrained firm needs more cash to buy inputs, it is forced to use less inputs and cut down its sales. These decreases are then amplified by a reduction in the credit available from suppliers (leverage effect). An initial drop of 0.7 units without this leverage effect translates into an effect of 1.0 unit with leverage. Therefore, the drop in inputs equals (1.0)/(0.7) = 1.43 times the initial effect because of this amplification.
Our second main assumption is that the share of inputs that customers can finance on credit from suppliers should be higher when costumers are more profitable. As we document in SectionIII.B, there are strong seasonal patterns in the cash flows of a broad range of U.S firms. Firms typically have main periods (quarters), during which their cash flows are predicted to be significantly stronger. Since the financing from suppliers is very short term, firms have more cash flows to repay suppliers in their main period and can finance a larger share of goods on credit during this period.
Returning to the numerical example above, suppose a customer can buy 35% of its inputs on credit from suppliers when inside its main quarter, rather than 30% (fraction outside of the main quarter). Then the same initial 1.0 unit change in inputs (unlevered) translates into a stronger immediate change in inputs and sales during the main period. The short-term change in inputs is (1.0)/ (0.65) = 1.54 units inside the main period, rather than (1.0)/ (0.7) = 1.43 units outside the main period. Intuitively, the firm can more aggressively leverage its short-term investment with short-term funding from suppliers during its main period. Consequently, the short-term investment response of the firm to a given economic shock should be more levered during its main period.
These ideas lead to the following predictions for firms that face binding constraints on the financing of short-term investments. The immediate percentage response of short-term investments and production (sales) to shocks should be stronger during a firm's main period. These levered responses should be present for any economic shock that affects short-term investment (e.g., shifts in demand conditions). However, these effects only amplify firms’ initial (unlevered) responses to shocks; they should therefore be limited when these initial responses are not significant.
We then examine the implications of this multiplier for the propagation of shocks over time. The starting point is that cuts in short-term investments today translate into immediate reductions in firm sales and cash flows, reducing internal funds available to fund operations moving forward. Lower internal funds in subsequent periods then lead to further reductions in firms’ short-term investments and sales in the future, propagating the effects of funding constraints over time. We also build on previous work analyzing the implications of financing frictions (Bernanke, Gertler, and Gilchrist (1999)), and further assume that decreases in firms’ internal resources adversely affect new borrowing terms.16
We next consider the differential propagation of shocks that hit inside versus outside a firm's main period. A firm that is hit by a shock inside its main period (say firmiin periodt) should observe a stronger propagation effect relative to a similar firm that was outside of its main period during periodt(say firmj). This differential propagation effect is a consequence of the higher immediate drop in production capacity for firmirelative to firmj, which leads to a stronger decline in internal funds and net worth for firmirelative to firmjin future periods.
However, this stronger propagation effect for firmidoes not necessarily mean that future short-term investment and sales will decrease more for firmithan for firmj. As we move forward in time, firmjwill eventually move into its main period (say periodt+ 1). If the shock is persistent in nature (such as an oil price shock), then there is a direct effect in periodt+ 1 that is stronger for firmj(now inside its main period) than for firmi. Future short-term investment and sales will decrease relatively more for firmithan for firmjif the propagation effect is stronger than the direct effect.
If propagation effects are sufficiently strong, our framework leads to the following key implication: shocks that initially hit firms inside their main period can have stronger percentage effects on the short-term investment and production (sales) not only contemporaneously, but also in subsequent periods. This persistence in the multiplier is a consequence of the propagation of shocks over time through endogenous changes in net worth. Responses that are initially more levered translate into more levered effects in subsequent periods.
While the discussion above focuses on firms that rely on working capital financing from suppliers, many firms also raise this type of funding from banks or other lenders (e.g., lines of credit). In principle, the predictions above could also be relevant for firms that rely on short-term debt. However, in contrast with supplier financing, not all short-term debt scales up with firms’ short-term investments in the way described above. For example, consider a firm selling goods on credit (receivables) that relies on a line of credit to fund its input costs and faces a borrowing limit. If this limit is predetermined and not shaped by the firms’ current profitability, the firm will not be able to finance a larger share of its inputs on credit during its main period (as in the example above). Supplier financing is different because firms constantly raise new short-term funding from suppliers, with new terms, as they buy new inputs. Therefore, our predictions should be mostly relevant for firms that rely strongly on supplier financing.
article.II. Empirical Strategy:
We build on the predictions discussed in SectionIto develop our empirical approach. We examine the differential percentage response of firms’ short-term investment and sales to shocks that hit during firms’ main quarter. The idea is to contrast the response over time to an economic shock that hits the firm (say firmi, the treated firm) inside its main quarter to the response to the same shock that hits a similar firm (say firmj, the control firm) outside of its main quarter. We label this difference as the main quarter effect. Our empirical approach to isolate the working capital multiplier is to examine if significant main quarter effects exist in both firms’ immediate as well as subsequent responses to shocks.
While the presence of a main quarter effect in firms’ immediate responses to shocks is an important prediction of the multiplier we analyze, this pattern could be driven by other factors. For example, even without frictions in working capital financing, firms might have stronger immediate responses to economic shocks such as input price increases during their main quarter. In contrast, main quarter effects in both immediate and subsequent quarters would imply a strongerpropagationover time for shocks that hit in the main quarter. The intuition comes from a key point discussed in SectionI. If firmj(the control firm above) is outside its main quarter when a shock hits (say quartert), it eventually will move into its main quarter in a subsequent period (say quartert+ 1). Hence, a stronger response to the shock by firmi(treated firm) in quartertimplies that the direct effect of the shock should be stronger for firmj(control firm) in quartert+ 1. Recall that the two firms are assumed to be similar and differ only in terms of the timing of their main quarter. Firmjis therefore in a similar position in quartert+ 1 to firmiin quartert. Going back to the example above, the effect of higher input prices should be stronger for firmiin quartertand firmjin quartert+ 1. But suppose we find that firmialso shows a greater response to the shock in quartert+ 1, relative to firmj. Such a result would imply a propagation of the shock over time.
The argument above does not rely on assumptions about the persistence of the shock over time (between quarterstandt+ 1). For example, suppose that the shock is not persistent, so that the effect of the shock on short-term investment fades over time. In that case, firmjmay show a weaker response to the shock in timet+ 1, when compared to firmi’s response in timet. But notice that our propagation test compares the response of firmito firmjin timet+ 1. Limited shock persistence alone cannot generate a stronger response of firmiin timet+ 1.
This propagation effect is consistent with the credit multiplier, as endogenous changes in net worth propagate the effect of the shock over time. However, in principle, this propagation effect could be driven by alternative mechanisms. For example, consider an input price shock that has a stronger immediate effect on firm sales in the main quarter. If the firm fires some workers as its sales decline, the initial effect of the shock on employment could drive its propagation over time. To address this concern, we examine whether propagation effects are concentrated among firms that rely strongly on credit from their suppliers. We also refine our approach to incorporate additional contrasts between effects for short- versus longer-term investments, as well as contrasts between different types of firms and shocks. These refinements allow us to further address potential concerns with our empirical approach. After discussing our results, we turn to challenges alternative mechanisms face in explaining these differences in the importance of propagation effects.
The analysis of main quarter effects requires comparing similar firms hit by shocks inside versus outside their main quarter. To isolate these effects, we analyze recurring shocks that are largely unpredictable, which are shocks that have a constant probability over time (in each quartert) and cannot be predicted using past information (known prior to the realization of the shock in quartert). Such shocks allow us to eliminate differences between firms hit inside their main quarter (treated firms) and firms hit outside their main quarter (control firms). For example, suppose we study a shock affecting firms in Q4 and contrast firms hit inside their main quarter with firms whose main quarter is Q3. Denote firms with their main quarter in calendar quarters Q4 and Q3 as Q4- and Q3-type firms, respectively. It is possible that Q4-type firms are systematically different from Q3-type firms in a way that could contaminate our identification strategy. For example, Q4-type firms are larger in our data and shocks could have different persistence for larger firms. However, because we userecurringshocks that are largelyunpredictable, our approach should address such potential issues. Given we analyze shocks over time, Q4-type firms have the same chance of being hit by a shock in their main quarter as Q3-type firms. Similarly, these two types of firms have the same probability of being hit by shocks outside their main quarter. Therefore, by examining a large sample of unpredictable shocks, the assignment of treated and controls firms should be random, and our treated and control groups should be virtually identical.17
An important issue in the implementation of our strategy is the choice of recurring and unpredictable shocks to analyze. The effects we want to isolate should be particularly relevant for shocks that more strongly affect firms’ short-term investments in the absence of any leverage from suppliers (these effects amplify firms’ unlevered responses). In particular, our theoretical framework predicts that shocks to input prices (production costs) should matter the most. Higher input prices increase the cash required by constrained firms to buy the same inputs, forcing these firms to scale down their inputs and production. Alternatively, adverse shocks to demand conditions do not lead to direct increases in the costs of inputs. When economic shocks have a smaller (unlevered) effect on firms’ short-term investments, the multiplier effect will be harder to detect in the data.
In selecting the economic shocks for our analysis, we build on the following considerations. Ideally, we would like to analyze shocks associated with changes in production costs (supply shocks) as our results should be stronger for such shocks. However, while in principle we could refine our shocks to attempt to isolate pure supply shocks, our empirical approach requires the analysis of a broad range of economic shocks. As described in SectionII.A, if we restrict our analysis to a small subset of shocks, the groups of treated and control firms (hit inside vs. outside the main quarter) will not be comparable and our identification strategy will not be valid. We also note that our analysis does not require isolating pure shocks to production costs. To the extent that the shocks in our analysis capture changes in other conditions, the mechanism that we want to isolate will be muted.
Motivated by these considerations, we construct a broad sample of industry shocks that change firms’ production costs. For each industry shock, we contrast the response of same-industry firms inside and outside their main quarter at the time of the shock. This procedure ensures that we are comparing firm responses to the same shock. When searching for industry shocks, we focus on shocks that are driven by aggregate observable conditions such as oil shocks or GDP fluctuations and are hard to predict. These shocks are largely unpredictable, recurring, and comparable over time, which are the basic features of industry shocks required by our identification strategy. To ensure that the shocks are associated with significant changes in production costs, in our main analysis we focus on industry shocks using oil prices (see SectionIII.C). We are not trying to suggest that oil prices should affect firms only through input prices. Rather, we are saying that the multiplier effects that we are trying to measure will be stronger when oil prices do change firms’ input prices. For example, changes in oil prices can also affect firms through changes in demand conditions.
In principle, the industry shocks that we analyze could affect firms’ short-term investment and sales through a range of mechanisms. These shocks should affect the decisions of firms that are not exposed to the working capital credit multiplier. They can be correlated with or partially driven by changes in the conditions faced by firms’ competitors, suppliers, and customers. Our identification strategy builds on the idea that these alternative mechanisms would not lead to all of the predictions that we examine in our analysis. In particular, these alternative mechanisms should not lead to propagation effects that are stronger when shocks hit in the main quarter, and that are concentrated on firms that rely strongly on credit from suppliers. As described above, we further refine our approach to examine differences between effects for short- versus longer-term investments, as well as contrasts across different types of firms and shocks. These refinements allow us to further address potential concerns that alternative mechanisms drive our results on propagation effects.
article.III. Data, Variables, and Motivating Pat:
We focus on listed firms because our analysis requires using firms’ current and historical quarterly financial statements. We start with data from Compustat's North America Fundamentals Quarterly. Following standard practice in the literature, we exclude financial firms (SIC codes 6000 to 6999) and regulated utilities (SIC codes 4900 to 4999). In our main analyses, we drop the largest firms in our data (top 33%) in terms of size, measured using the one-quarter lag of total assets (atq). Previous research suggests that the importance of payables among the largest firms is driven less by financing needs. Instead, it is motivated by economic considerations such as ensuring the quality provided by suppliers (Klapper, Laeven, and Rajan (2012)). In theInternet Appendix, we show the average importance of payables by size decile for a broad sample of firms. Firms in the bottom of the size distribution rely significantly on payables and this importance declines with size until the top three size deciles, for which the importance of payables becomes relevant again and is potentially driven by these alternative economic considerations. Motivated by this pattern, which is consistent with previous research (Murfin and Njoroge (2015)), we drop firms in the top size tercile. Our main results are similar if use alternative cutoffs for these large firms such as the top decile or quartile (see theInternet Appendix).
We implement our main tests using oil price shocks to ensure that the shocks are associated with significant changes in production costs (see SectionII). For this same reason, we focus on industries (three-digit SIC codes) with a negative cash-flow exposure to oil price increases (see SectionIII.C). We consider broader samples and shocks below. Finally, the construction of the variables used in our analysis requires that firms have nonmissing quarterly data on cash flows for the past five years (20 quarters). We restrict our sample to firms with such nonmissing data and limit our sample period to 1980 to 2015. After imposing these restrictions, we arrive at the main sample used in our results, which covers 3,170 firms and counts 46,185 observations over this 36-year period. TableIprovides summary statistics for this main sample.
We capture firms’ short-term investments in their operations using receivables and inventories. For example, one can think of a construction company paying for costs prior to billing (receivables) and a manufacturing firm completing an order prior to delivery (inventories). Panel A of TableIshows that these two assets combined represent approximately one-third of firms’ total assets in our main sample. In comparison, this magnitude is similar to the average ratio of net property, plant, and equipment (Net PPE) over total assets. This importance of receivables and inventories is similar in broader samples such as all U.S. listed nonfinancial firms between 1980 and 2015.
We document the existence of strong predictability of firm profitability within a year and link it to firms’ reliance on credit from suppliers. Firms have main quarters each calendar year in which their profitability is predicted to be significantly larger. We capture this pattern using the variableMain Quarter, an indicator that equals one in firms’ most profitable quarter. We use historical data to identify firms’ main quarter which captures seasonality in firms’ operating conditions. For each calendar year, we use data on the previous five calendar years (20 quarters) to identify a firm's main quarter. We follow the approach in Chang et al. (2017) and first rank these 20 quarters in terms of their profitability (Cash Flow). Note that each quarter (Q1, Q2, Q3, and Q4) is ranked five times and, for example, can be in the five top positions among the 20 quarters (ranks one to four). We then estimate the average rank for each quarter and define the main quarter as the quarter with the lowest average rank (highest average position). The use of the average rank, as opposed to the average cash flow, reduces the influence of individual quarters with abnormal cash flows in this classification.
Panel A of TableIIshows that the main quarter variable (constructed using historical data) predicts large differences in firm profitability within a quarter-industry. The results are based on linear regressions predictingCash FlowusingMain Quarterand industry-quarter fixed effects (three-digit SIC code). We relateMain Quarterin quartertto both contemporaneousCash Flowand the sum ofCash Flowin quarterstandt+ 1. Our regressions include fixed effects for firms with different calendar quarters (e.g., Q1) as their main quarter (four firm types) because these firm types could predict systematic differences in firm profitability. We scale the coefficient onMain Quarterby dividing the estimated coefficient by the mean of the outcome being analyzed in the main sample (see TableI). We find that firms’ main quarter is associated with predictable differences in profitability that are economically large for both definitions ofCash Flow(45% to 80% of the mean of cash flows). These results confirm the view that the seasonality we analyze does predict cash flows within a year. TheInternet Appendixalso shows that firms in our main sample have main quarters well distributed throughout the year.
We focus on the differences in seasonality within an industry (three-digit SIC code) because we predict economic shocks at this same industry level. To contrast firms’ responses to a same shock and estimate main quarter effects, we rely on variation inMain Quarterwithin a same industry-quarter. An important question is what drives these significant differences in seasonality within an industry (three-digit SIC code). Note that conditional on a same three-digit SIC industry, there can be significant differences across firms in their specific product markets (subject to different cycles). The location of firms can also be an important source of variation in seasonality. Markets can be geographically segmented (e.g., services) and firms’ local conditions can shape seasonality for other reasons such as weather patterns within a year (e.g., construction firms operating in Illinois vs. Texas). Indeed, in theInternet Appendix, we show that a combination of more detailed industry definitions and firm locations can explain a large part of within-industry differences in seasonality. This analysis highlights why firms inside versus outside their main quarter at agiven point in timeexhibit differences. Our empirical approach relies on these differences in firm product markets and locations as source of variation forMain Quarterwithin each shock and builds on the following idea. As we look at several shocks over time, the set of firms hit inside versus outside their main quarter across all these shocks should become similar (see SectionII).
We then link this seasonality in profitability to firms’ reliance on credit from suppliers. Recall that the mechanism we propose in SectionIbuilds on the following condition. Customers are using all of the credit available from suppliers, that is, they face a binding borrowing limit, and the share of their short-term investment that they can finance on credit from suppliers is larger in their main quarter, when they have more cash flows to repay suppliers. If these patterns hold in the data, firms’ payables should increase relative to their short-term investment (inventories and receivables) during their main quarter. We directly examine this prediction. Intuitively, this prediction captures the idea that customers are more levered when buying goods on credit in their main quarter. Only firms that rely significantly on supplier financing are potentially exposed to this binding borrowing limit from suppliers. We focus on differential patterns for these firms.
We capture the importance of supplier financing using the ratio of accounts payable to sales.18As a first step, we rank firms into three terciles usingSupplier Financing, the average value of this ratio in the previous four quarters (sorted by year). We use the ratio of accounts payable to sales because this ratio captures the importance of these liabilities as a share of firms’ overall production.19We then separately analyze the top two groups of firms, which we refer to as middle- and top-supplier-financing firms. Specifically, we examine the growth of payables relative to working capital assets and check if this growth differentially increases for each of these groups during the main quarter, controlling for basic firm characteristics (size, age, andQ) in some of the results. We relate firm borrowing from suppliers at the end of quarterstandt+ 1 to whether quartertis a main quarter. This timing captures the response of firms’ borrowing to their main quarter. Recall that firms with their main quarter inthave significantly stronger profitability in quarterstandt+ 1 (Panel A of TableII).
Panel B of TableII(columns (1) and (2)) shows that top-supplier-financing firms borrow more aggressively from suppliers during their main quarter, that is, their payables increase relative to their working capital assets (receivables and inventories). This effect is not economically or statistically important for the middle-supplier-financing group. Notice that in these regressions the coefficients are measured relative to the low-supplier-financing group (bottom tercile).
The results above support the prediction for top-supplier-financing firms. We note that this empirical pattern could be driven by alternative explanations and is not necessarily an indication of funding constraints from suppliers. For example, this pattern could be driven by a pecking order, whereby firms rely first on internal funds or short-term debt and rely more on suppliers during their main quarter as they need additional funds during this period. However, this prediction represents an important consistency check for our analysis. The mechanism we propose in SectionIshould be present and detected in the data only when firms satisfy this prediction. Our identification approach to isolate the importance of this mechanism is described in SectionII.
A comparison of the magnitudes across the results in Panels A and B of TableIIis also consistent with the idea that firms’ higher cash flows in their main quarter allows them to borrow more from suppliers in this period. The results in Panel A (column (2)) imply that cash flows increase for these firms by the equivalent of 5% of their payables. The results in Panel B suggest that these same firms use approximately 1.5% more credit from suppliers during their main quarter.
Since this pattern concentrates on top-supplier-financing firms, we next focus on contrasting this group with all other firms (the bottom and middle terciles). Columns (3) and (4) in Panel B show the previous effect using this contrast. In these regressions, the coefficient on the top-supplier-financing group is measured relative to the two other groups (bottom and middle terciles). The coefficients are similar to those in columns (1) and (2). Finally, Panel C shows that we also find similar patterns when we examine the growth of payables relative to sales, as opposed to working capital assets. In theInternet Appendix, we provide a description of the industries covered in this group of top-supplier-financing firms.
An important step in our analysis is the construction of industry shocks. As discussed in SectionII, we construct a broad sample of industry shocks and focus on recurring shocks that are driven by aggregate observable conditions such as oil shocks or GDP fluctuations and that are hard to predict. In our main results, we analyze industry shocks using oil prices and focus on industries (three-digit SIC codes) with a negative cash-flow exposure to oil price increases. We follow this approach to ensure that the shocks are associated with significant changes in production costs. However, we also consider a range of other approaches to construct industry shocks, such as predicting changes in industry cash flows using GDP shocks and using predicted effects from oil prices for industries with positive oil betas. In addition, in the context of industries with negative exposure to oil shocks, we decompose the effects of oil prices on industries’ revenues and profitability. As we move into shocks that are less likely to capture shifts in production costs and more likely to reflect other conditions such as product-market demand, our mechanism should become weaker and less likely to be detected in the data.
When measuring industry oil betas (three-digit SIC codes), we start by estimating firm-level oil betas using a regression ofΔCashFlow$\Delta CashFlow$onOil Price Growth, whereΔCashFlow$\Delta CashFlow$is the difference betweenCash Flowin quartertand its average value between quarterst− 1 andt− 4.Oil Price Growthis the change in the average price of oil (deflated) between quarterstandt− 1 and captures quarterly innovations in oil prices.20When measuring quarterly oil prices, we start with monthly data on spot crude oil prices (West Texas Intermediate (WTI) dollars per barrel) and then deflate prices and compute average prices. We use all years of available data for each firm and estimate industry betas as the average of these firm-level betas in the industry. We construct industry-level shocks asInd Shock = Oil Price Growth × Oil Exposure, whereOil Exposureis the value of the estimated industry oil beta and is fixed over the sample period. These industry-level shocks are predicted shocks based on the differential sensitivity of industry cash flows to innovations in oil prices. We construct industry shocks using GDP growth similarly (see SectionV.Afor more details). As previously discussed, when constructing our main sample, we keep only industries with a negative value for this industry beta (constant over time).
In theInternet Appendix, we show that the main industry shocks used in our analysis have strong effects on firm cash flows, and that these shocks have significant effects on firms’ operating performance. Another important advantage of analyzing oil shocks is that previous research provides direct evidence that these shocks are largely unpredictable. For example, when discussing evidence on the (lack) of predictability of oil prices, Hamilton (2009) explains that this evidence shows that “the real price of oil is not easy to forecast” and that is “not at all naïve to offer as a forecast [for the future price of oil] whatever the price currently happens to be.”
As discussed in SectionII, if these shocks are largely unpredictable, the sets of firms hit by a shock inside versus outside their main quarter should have very similar distributions for firm characteristics. Panel C of TableIIprovides direct support for this idea by analyzing four basic firm characteristics (Size,Age,Q, andCash Flow) in the year prior to oil price shocks. We contrast firms hit by shocks inside versus outside their main quarter, where shocks are values above the median for the absolute value ofOil Price Growth. We confirm that these two subsamples are very similar across multiple firm characteristics. TheInternet Appendixshows the same patterns using alternative definitions for these shocks and confirms that these differences across subsamples are statistically insignificant. These results support our argument in SectionIIthat our research design leads to treated and control groups that are randomly assigned, that is, similar sets of firms that are different only in terms of when they are hit by shocks (inside vs. outside the main quarter).
Our empirical analysis builds on several points about hedging and the lead-lag structure of shocks and production that we clarify here. First, we rely on the assumption that firms cannot fully hedge their exposure to these shocks. Specifically, our analysis is designed to isolate a direct effect of industry shocks on top-supplier-financing firms, which should not be present if these firms can completely hedge their exposure to oil price shocks. Previous research provides direct evidence that smaller firms typically do not hedge such shocks fully (e.g., Froot, Scharfstein, and Stein (1990), and Stulz (1996)). This research also discusses intuitive explanations for this behavior. For example, Rampini, Sufi, and Viswanathan (2014) provide evidence that airlines hedge only a part of their fuel-cost exposure to oil prices (with derivatives) and that oil-price hedging is less common when firms are likely to face financing constraints. They emphasize the point that firms hedging these price shocks need to make significant payments if commodity prices do not adversely affect them, and financing constraints might limit firms’ ability and willingness to do so. In a scenario in which the credit multiplier we analyze is relevant, top-supplier-financing firms face funding constraints and these constraints limit their ability to hedge. This view that hedging is costly and limited among firms facing funding issues is also consistent with anecdotal evidence.21
In terms of the lead-lag structure of shocks and production, we rely on the following timeline. Shocks initiated in quartertimmediately affect firms’ new short-term investments (receivables and inventories), which can then significantly influence their production capacity between quarterstandt+ 1. This timeline builds on the idea that firms make significant short-term investments in these assets during the six months prior to the collection of cash from sales. Both previous research and anecdotal evidence support this timing. For example, Petersen and Rajan (1997) note that credit from suppliers is used largely to finance such short-term investments, as this allows firms to match the maturity of assets and liabilities. Since trade credit has very short maturity (Klapper, Laeven, and Rajan (2012)), the demand for this credit must come from significant short-term investments with an equally short maturity. While firms can make investments in these assets (e.g., inventories) with longer maturities, as we discuss in SectionIV.C, these longer-term investments do not lead to the predictions that we analyze. Hence, the predictions of our mechanism should be present in the data only if these investments with short maturities are important.
article.IV. Main Results:
The outcomeΔYijt+k,t+lmeasures the response of firms’ short-term investment or sales during a period after a shock in quartert. We first measure the immediate effect of the shock during the first semester (average between quarterstandt+ 1, orΔYijt,t+1). We then analyze the effect of the shock on the subsequent semester (average between quarterst+ 2 andt+ 3, orΔYijt+2,t+3). These two periods cover firms’ entire annual responses to the shocks. We examine the percentage responses of firms’ short-term investment and sales (see SectionsIandII) usingΔlog(ST Assets)ijt+k,t+1andΔlog(Sales)ijt+k,t+1, respectively, whereST Assets = Receivables + Inventories. Sales,Receivables, andInventoriesdenotes a firm's total sales, accounts receivables, and inventories, respectively, and are all measured using quarterly data.
The coefficient of interest isβ1${\beta _1}$. We measure the immediate differential effect of the shock when using the outcomeΔYijt,t+1, and the differential propagation of the shock when using the outcome variableΔYijt+2,t+3.As explained above, in the presence of the working capital credit multiplier, shocks that initially hit firms inside their main period can have stronger percentage effects on short-term investment and production (sales) not only contemporaneously (immediate effect), but also in subsequent periods (propagation effect). This can lead the coefficientβ1${\beta _1}$to be positive for both outcomes (ΔYijt,t+1andΔYijt+2,t+3). As we explain in SectionII, this pattern (positiveβ1${\beta _1}$for both outcomes) must necessarily imply a propagation of shocks over time. Our focus on these propagation effects helps us distinguish the working capital credit multiplier from other mechanisms.
Note thatMain Quarterindicates whether the firm is inside its main quarter at the time when the shock hits (quartert). Also note that industry shocks and industry × quarter fixed effects (θjt${\theta _{jt}}$) are defined at the same level (three-digit SIC codes), which ensures thatβ1${\beta _1}$captures differences in firms’ responses to the same industry shock. Recall that in our main approach, industry shocks are given byInd Shock = Oil Price Growth × Oil Exposure. The controls in the estimation of equation (1) includeMain Quarterand its interaction withOil Price GrowthandOil Exposure. We also control for the average value ofQbetween quarterst− 1 andt− 4 to capture potential expectations of such shocks.
As motivated in SectionIII.B, we focus our analysis on the top-supplier-financing firms, and contrast patterns for this group with those for other firms (bottom and middle terciles). Only firms relying significantly on supplier financing are potentially exposed to the mechanism we propose in SectionI. Importantly, in SectionIII.Bwe show that only top supplier firms use more leverage from suppliers during their main quarter, a necessary condition for the presence of the mechanism we propose. We first separately estimate equation (1) for the three terciles and then contrast these effects for top-supplier-financing firms versus other firms.
As discussed in SectionII, an important aspect of our research design is that the sets of firms hit by industry shocks inside and outside their main quarter should be very similar as we analyze a large number of recurring shocks. Indeed, in the context of the oil shocks used in our main analysis, SectionIII.Cprovides direct support for this prediction. In our analysis, we control for some potential differences across firms hit by shocks inside versus outside the main quarter. Specifically, we classify firms with a main quarter in Q1, Q2, Q3, and Q4 as Q1-, Q2-, Q3-, and Q4-type firms, respectively. We then include indicators for these four firm types (e.g., Q2-type firms) as controls. These controls are included in a symmetric way toMain Quarter, and we add interactions between these indicators and the variables used to construct industry shocks as control variables. Consistent with our research design, in theInternet Appendix, we show that our results remain virtually unchanged without these controls.22
This section presents our main results by analyzing firms’ differential responses to industry shocks when these shocks hit in the main quarter, estimated following the approach in SectionIV.A. TableIIIreports the results for firm sales. We first show these effects separately for firms in each of the three terciles of supplier financing (Panel A). The estimated coefficient onInd Shock × MQuarteris scaled to better capture its magnitude. We multiply this coefficient by the product of the standard deviation ofOil Price Growthand 0.01 (average absolute oil exposure in the sample). This allows us to interpret the results as capturing the effect of an industry shock implied by a typical oil price shock on an industry with significant (negative) exposure to oil prices. Since only top-supplier-financing firms are potentially exposed to the mechanism we propose (see SectionIV.A), we start by analyzing the results for these firms (columns (1) and (4)). We find that when industry shocks hit in the main quarter, their (percentage) effect on firm sales is stronger both in the initial semester (column (1)) and the subsequent semester (column (4)). These results support the predictions of our mechanism outlined in SectionI. In particular, our empirical approach detects a significant propagation effect when industry shocks hit these firms in their main quarter. Estimates in columns (1) and (4) imply significant effects with the same sign in both semesters for top-supplier-financing firms. A one-standard-deviation shock to oil prices is associated with effects between 2% and 3% of firm sales in both periods. This pattern helps us distinguish the working capital multiplier from other potential mechanisms.
In contrast, our empirical approach does not detect significant propagation effects in the responses of other firms (middle and bottom supplier financing) to these same shocks. While shocks that hit in the main quarter can affect these firms’ responses in one of the two semesters after the shock, we do not find a significant effect in the same direction in both periods. For example, shocks that hit in the main quarter have a weaker immediate effect (first semester) for bottom-supplier-financing firms (column (3)), but there is not a significant effect with the same sign during the subsequent semester (column (6)). As discussed in SectionII, there can be differences in firms’ immediate responses to shocks initiated in the main quarter. Our main approach to isolate the importance of the working capital multiplier is to examine the propagation of these initial effects over time. The results in TableIIIshow that we detect this propagation only in the group of firms that is potentially exposed to the multiplier.
Panel B explicitly contrasts the effects measured in Panel A for top supplier firms versus other firms and examines if there are differential propagation effects for top supplier firms. This test allows us to control for common patterns in the response of all firms to shocks. We implement this idea by estimating equation (1) using our overall sample (all subsamples in Panel A) and interacting the main effect of interest (Ind Shock × Main Quarter) withTop Supplier Fin, an indicator variable that equals one for top-supplier-financing firms. We also include interactions of all oil variables,Ind Shock,Main Quarter, and industry×quarter fixed effects withTop Supplier Finas controls. In some specifications, we also include controls for basic firm characteristics (Age,Size, andQ), measured as average values between quarterst− 1 andt− 4 and treated in a symmetric way toTop Supplier Fin. This specification allows us to control for differences across firms that can be explained by these basic firm characteristics as opposed to supplier financing. The estimated coefficients are scaled in the same way as in Panel A.
Columns (1) and (4) show that our results for top-supplier-financing firms continue to hold when estimated using this approach. The results confirm that main quarter effects are stronger for top-supplier-financing firms in both the first and second semesters after the shock, and document the importance of the propagation of industry shocks for these firms. As in Panel A, we do not detect significant propagation effects for firms that are not top-supplier-financing firms. This is now captured by the coefficients forInd Shock × Main Quarterin columns (1) and (4). In columns (2), (3), (5), and (6), we show that these effects continue to hold when we control for basic firm characteristics that could be related toTop Supplier Fin.
TableIVshows similar results for the response of firms’ short-term investment (receivables and inventories). Panels A and B show that the findings above for the response of firm sales are qualitatively and quantitatively similar to those for short-term investment. The results also detect a significant propagation effect for short-term investment that is present only for top-supplier-financing firms and is differentially stronger for these firms.
Our approach to isolate propagation effects is to compare similar firms being hit inside (treated firms) versus outside (control firms) their main quarter. As discussed in SectionII, this assumption is valid if oil shocks are unpredictable. Moreover, this idea is consistent with our data, as treated and control firms are virtually identical. Our analysis further supports this idea by showing that the previous results in TablesIIIandIVare not sensitive to the inclusion of controls for potential firm characteristics correlated withMain Quarter. First, in theInternet Appendixwe show that our results remain virtually the same when we exclude the controls for the four different firm types (Q1-, Q2-, Q3-, and Q4-type firms) used in our main specification. For example, if oil shocks are seasonal, our results could capture systematic differences across firms with main quarters in different parts of the year. This finding suggests that this concern is not important. The estimates in Panel B of TablesIIIandIV(columns (2), (3), (5), and (6)) show results after controlling for basic firm characteristics (Age,Size, andQ) that could be related toMain Quarter. This analysis includes interactions of these characteristics withInd Shockin an analogous way toMain Quarter. The stability of our results after the inclusion of these controls provides further evidence supporting the idea above that our research design eliminates potential differences across firms hit inside versus outside their main quarter. This evidence is also consistent with the point that is hard to predict shocks to oil prices (SectionIII.C).
We next examine whether the previous propagation effects are stronger for firms’ short-term investment relative to their long-term investment. The asymmetric importance of short-term investment effects is a central aspect of the mechanism we propose in SectionI, which focuses on working capital financing issues and funding of short-term assets such as payables and inventories. We start by presenting the main intuitions for this prediction. We describe how these intuitions can be formally modeled in theAppendix.
We first consider firms’ immediate (first-semester) response to shocks. As we discuss in SectionI, the response of short-term investment to shocks is stronger in the main quarter because customers can finance a larger share of their short-term investment on credit from suppliers during this period. In principle, one could apply a similar idea to the funding of firms’ long-term investment. If firms finance a share of their long-term investment using new credit, this can also lead to a levered response of firms’ long-term investment to shocks.
The key point to note is that, in contrast with short-term investments, the share of long-term investments financed on credit should not be significantly larger during firms’ main period. As Petersen and Rajan (1997) highlight, it is natural to expect firms to match the maturity of assets and liabilities and finance long-term (short-term) investments with long-term (short-term) funding. If this is the case, firms financing a share of their long-term investment on credit will rely on long-term debt. While firms’ ability to raise short-term credit from suppliers should be shaped by temporary differences in their cash flows during the main quarter (SectionI), this should not be the case for long-term debt, which is paid over future periods. Empirically, if the mechanism we propose is also present for long-term investments, we should expect to see a positive link between firms’ main quarter and their long-term leverage (ratio of long-term debt over long-term assets). In theInternet Appendix, we document that this pattern is not present, which provides additional support to the arguments above. These results imply that our previous results analyzing firms’ immediate (first-semester) responses to shocks should be stronger for short-term investments.
When describing the propagation of shocks over time in SectionI, we highlight the role of changes in firms’ internal funds. As firms cut their short-term investments and sales in their initial response to shocks, this reduces the funds available to fund operations in subsequent periods. In principle, lower internal funds in subsequent periods should limit firms’ ability to fund both short- and long-term investments. To illustrate why this propagation effect might asymmetrically affect firms’ short-term investment, we build on previous work analyzing the implications of financing frictions (Bernanke, Gertler, and Gilchrist (1999)) and assume that drops in firms’ internal resources adversely affect new borrowing terms. For example, lenders might charge higher spreads or impose stricter borrowing limits on new credit when borrowers have less internal funds.23When firms finance a share of their short-term investment on credit from suppliers, reductions in firms’ internal funds will immediately increase the marginal cost of expanding this investment. Intuitively, these short-term investments are exposed to changes in refinancing terms for this short-term credit.
In contrast, these increased refinancing costs are less relevant for the cost of expanding long-term investment. As we discuss above, when financing expansions to their long-term investment on credit, firms should rely on long-term debt. Because of issuance costs, firms do not raise long-term debt in every period that they make long-term investments (which contrasts with the need to refinance short-term funding every period). Therefore, the immediate effect of new refinancing terms on the cost of long-term investment is more limited. This asymmetry in the exposure to refinancing terms induces a substitution across these two types of investment. Intuitively, after drops in firms’ internal funds, there is an increase in the relative cost of financing short-term investments and a substitution away from these investments into long-term investments. This substitution amplifies the response of short-term investments over time, while it mitigates the response of longer-term investment. TheAppendixprovides more details on these points.
We analyze this prediction by estimating our previous results (TablesIIIandIV) using the difference between firms’ short- and long-term investment as the outcome and checking if the previously documented patterns also hold in this case. Specifically, we first measure percentage changes to firms’ short-term investments (receivables and inventories) in TableIV. We then examine changes to capital expenditures usingΔInvijt+k,t+l, the average value ofInvbetween quarterst+kandt+lminus its average value between quarterst− 1 andt− 4, whereInvis the ratio of capital expenditures to the one-quarter lag of net PPE. This last variable is also measured as a percentage of PPE. When analyzing the differential response of firms’ short-term investment, we use the difference between these two outcome variables.24
TableVreports the results, which are qualitatively and quantitatively similar to those in TablesIIIandIV. Our empirical approach detects propagation effects that are significant only for top supplier firms (Panel A). These propagation effects for top supplier financing remain similar when we analyze the differential response of these firms versus other firms (middle- and bottom-supplier-financing firms) to shocks (Panel B). Moreover, these patterns remain stable as we control for differences in basic firm characteristics across these groups of firms. Overall, this evidence confirms that the propagation effects that we emphasize are stronger for firms’ short-term investment relative to their long-term investment.
We provide additional evidence that supports the role of the mechanism we propose in SectionIas an explanation for the previous propagation effects. In principle, as we discuss in SectionII, the propagation effects that we document could be driven by alternative mechanisms. For example, consider an input price shock that has a stronger immediate effect on firm sales in the main quarter. If the firm fires some workers as it cuts back its sales, the initial effect of the shock on employment could drive its propagation over time. Alternatively, stronger initial effects on sales might lead the firm to lose some customers who do not switch back to the firm in future periods, translating into a propagation of initial conditions over time.
These alternative mechanisms are likely to be associated with drops in long-term investment. As firms persistently use fewer workers or face a reduced demand from customers, their return on long-term investments should decline. In contrast, the mechanism we propose can rationalize a pattern where drops in sales over time are associated with a muted response or even an increase in long-term investment. In our mechanism, the reduction in sales is associated with a substitution away from short-term investments and into longer-term investments (see SectionIV.B).
To examine these possibilities, we estimate our main results using long-term investment as the outcome variable (ΔInvijt+k,t+l). We replicate our results from Panel B of TableIII, which focuses on the differential patterns for top-supplier-financing firms. Panel A of TableVIshows the results. We find that our previous findings analyzing firms’ sales and short-term investments are not associated with economically or statistically significant effects for long-term investment. This evidence further supports the role of the mechanism we propose in driving our results.
We then examine an important prediction of our mechanism, namely, that our results for sales and short-term investment should be matched with similar percentage changes in customers’ credit from suppliers (accounts payable). In the mechanism we propose, firms finance a share of their short-term investment on credit and changes in this investment should be matched with comparable effects on payables. Moreover, as we explain in SectionIV.B, reductions in firms’ internal resources adversely affect terms of new borrowing. This change in borrowing terms amplifies the effects on payables over time (in the second semester after the shock), as firms experience changes in internal resources.
Panel B of TableVIreports the results analyzing these predictions. We replicate our previous results (Panel B of TableIII) using percentage changes in payables as the outcome variable (Δlog(Payables)ijt+k,t+l). This variable measures the average value of log(Payables)between quarterst+kandt+lminus its average value between quarterst− 1 andt− 4, wherePayablesis the firm's accounts payable. Consistent with the predictions from our mechanism, we find that our sales and short-term investment results are matched with qualitatively and quantitatively similar effects on payables. Moreover, these effects on payables become stronger over time (second semester after the shock). These findings are consistent with our mechanism.
article.V. Additional Results:
In this section, we study additional implications of our mechanism. These predictions correspond to firms’ responses to a broader range of economic shocks and the contrast between top-supplier-financing firms and firms that rely strongly on short-term debt.
We examine the previous effects using a broader range of economic shocks. As discussed in SectionII, the mechanism that we analyze predicts that these effects should be mostly relevant for shocks strongly associated with changes in production costs. We explore this intuition in three ways. First, we predict industry shocks using oil prices for positive-oil-beta industries. Oil shocks are less likely to matter through input costs for industries that have higher profits when oil prices increase. Second, we decompose the effect of oil prices on industry sales and profitability for negative oil beta industries. Since our effects should be most relevant for shocks associated with changes in production costs, we expect this decomposition to show stronger results when looking at profitability than when looking at sales.25Third, we predict industry shocks using GDP growth. When compared to the industry shocks used in our main results, that is, oil shocks for industries with negative betas, general shocks to GDP are less likely to capture shifts in production costs. Therefore, we should see muted responses of sales and short-term investment to these industry shocks driven by GDP growth.26
TableVIIpresents the results for the response of firm sales. The results in TableVIImatch all of the predictions above. We report scaled effects in an analogous way to TableIII, by multiplying the coefficients of interest by the standard deviation of the aggregate shock (oil or GDP shock) in the sample and the typical exposure of firms to these shocks, that is, average absolute value for the industry beta. Panel A shows that there are no significant propagation effects for top-supplier-financing firms in industries with positive oil beta. Panel B shows that for negative oil beta industries, the results are driven by the impact of oil prices on production costs. There are no propagation effects for sales shocks that are driven by changes in oil prices. There are also no significant propagation effects for industry shocks that arise from changes in GDP growth (Panels C and D), even when focusing on the main sample used in our previous results. TableVIIIanalyzes these same results for firms’ short-term investment. These results for short-term investment also match all the predictions described above.
We next compare our main results for top-supplier-financing firms with similar results for firms that rely strongly on short-term debt. As discussed in SectionI, our mechanism should be mostly important for firms that rely strongly on supplier financing.
