While many firms can raise working capital financing from banks or other lenders (e.g., lines of credit), not all short-term debt should scale up with firms’ inputs along the lines discussed in SectionI. For example, consider a firm that uses a line of credit to fund its input costs and faces a borrowing limit. If this limit is predetermined and not shaped by the firm's current profitability, the firm will not be able to finance a larger share of its inputs on credit during its main period (as predicted by the mechanism we analyze).
If our mechanism is present for top-short-term-debt firms, their short-term debt should increase relative to their short-term investment (inventories and receivables) during their main quarter. This necessary condition follows the same logic discussed in SectionIII.Bfor the case of credit from suppliers. In theInternet Appendix, we show that this condition is not supported by the data using a similar specification to the one in Panel B of TableII. Relatedly, in contrast with the results for top-supplier-financing firms in TableII, we show that the payables of top-short-term debt firms do not increase relative to their short-term investment during their main quarter. These results reinforce the point above that our main effects should not be relevant for top-short-term-debt firms.
TableIXexamines the importance of our results for top-short-term-debt firms. We first replicate our findings in Panel A of TablesIIIandIVusing the importance of short-term debt (as opposed to credit from suppliers). We define top-, middle-, and bottom-short-term-debt firms in an analogous way to top-, middle-, and bottom-supplier-financing firms using the ratio of short-term debt to sales. Panels A and C show these results for the responses of firm sales and short-term investments, respectively. Consistent with the discussion above, we do not detect significant propagation effects in response to shocks for top-short-term-debt firms. We also do not find these patterns for the other groups of firms, and these findings are similar for the response of sales and short-term investments.
We then examine whether our previous effects for top-supplier-financing firms are stronger than those for top-short-term-debt firms. Panels B and D report the results for the response of sales and short-term investment, respectively. We expand the specifications in Panel B of TablesIIIandIV, to includeTop STDand its interactions withInd Shock×Main Quarter, whereTop STDis an indicator for top-short-term-debt firms. Specifically, we includeTop STDin a symmetric way toTop Supplier Fin, and control for the interactions of all oil variables,Ind Shock, andMain QuarterwithTop STD. We also include interactions of industry × quarter fixed effects withTop Supplier Fin × Top STD. The results confirm that our previous findings for top-supplier-financing firms are differentially important when compared to the same results for top-short-term-debt firms. We also detect propagation effects when we analyze differences in the responses of these firms to industry shocks over time. Moreover, these patterns remain important and similar for the responses of both sales and short-term investment. Overall, these findings show that our results are not present for all firms that rely strongly on short-term funding. Rather, as predicted by our mechanism, these effects are present only when firms rely strongly on supplier financing.
article.VI. Alternative Interpretations:
We interpret our findings above as driven by funding frictions in the financing of short-term investments (receivables and payables) that limit firms’ production capacity. In this section, we discuss whether alternative explanations can plausibly explain our results. As we highlight in SectionIIand the discussion of our results, our main approach to isolate the importance of these funding frictions is to test for propagation effects of shocks. In principle, other factors could explain why firms’ immediate responses to shocks are significantly different during their main quarter. Our identification strategy focuses on the propagation of these initial effects from shocks over time.
A first potential concern with our evidence is that our results may not isolate the propagation of firms’ responses to shocks. For example, one might be concerned that our approach depends on assumptions about the persistence of economic shocks over time. As we highlight in SectionII, our approach does not rely on such assumptions. The key intuition for our test is that control firms initially hit by shocks outside of their main quarter eventually will move into their main quarter in a subsequent period. Hence, a stronger initial response to the shock by treated firms, inside their main quarter when the shock hits, implies that the direct effect of the shock should be stronger for control firms in subsequent quarters. However, we document that treated firms also show a greater response to shocks in subsequent quarters, relative to control firms. Our results therefore indicate a propagation of the shock over time.
Our interpretation builds on the assumption that treated and control firms in our analysis are similar to each other. Under this assumption, our results capture the incremental effect of shocks that hit in the firm's main quarter. As we explain in SectionII, since the oil shocks we analyze are hard to predict (see SectionIII.C), we expect the firms hit inside versus outside their main quarter to be similar. Indeed, consistent with this idea, SectionIII.Cshows that firms hit by oil shocks inside versus outside their main quarter are very similar in terms of multiple basic characteristics. In addition, SectionIV.Bdescribes how our main findings do not change as we control for potential differences across firms hit inside versus outside their main quarter. All of this evidence suggests that treated and control firms are in fact very similar to each other.
We interpret these propagation effects as evidence of a working capital credit multiplier. As firms’ initial responses to shocks reduce the internal resources available in future periods, this reduction adversely affects firms’ ability to fund their short-term investments (receivables and payables) and limits their production capacity. In principle, alternative mechanisms could also rationalize the propagation effects that we document. For example, consider an input price shock that has a stronger immediate effect on firms’ sales in the main quarter. If the firm fires some workers as its sales decline, the initial effect of the shock on employment could drive its propagation over time. Alternatively, stronger initial effects on sales might lead the firm to lose some customers who do not switch back to the firm in future periods, translating into a propagation of initial conditions over time. We believe our evidence is not consistent with these alternative explanations for the following reasons.
First, the propagation effects that we document are significant and differentially important only for firms that rely strongly on credit from suppliers. We show that these effects are not present for firms that do not rely strongly on credit from suppliers (SectionIV.B) or firms that rely strongly on short-term debt (SectionV.B). It is unclear why these alternative explanations would lead to propagation effects only in the specific subset of firms that are potentially exposed to our mechanism. We also find that these propagation effects are stronger for firms’ short-term investments, when compared to long-term investments (SectionIV.C). Moreover, these effects are not associated with economically or statistically significant changes in long-term investment (SectionIV.D). If drops in sales over time are driven by these alternative mechanisms, it is plausible to expect these effects to be associated with reductions in long-term investment. As firms persistently use fewer workers or face reduced demand from customers, their return on long-term investments should become lower. In contrast, the mechanism we propose naturally rationalizes a pattern whereby drops in sales over time are associated with a muted response of long-term investment (SectionIV.D). Also consistent with our mechanism, we detect significant propagation effects only for shocks more strongly associated with changes in production costs.
A related possibility is that firms’ internal resources lead to propagation effects by limiting firms’ ability to make long-term investments, as opposed to having direct effects on firms’ short-term investments and sales. For the same reasons listed above, we believe our evidence is not consistent with this explanation. In contrast with this interpretation, we find no significant propagation effects for firms’ long-term investment. In addition, the initial effects of shocks translate into immediate and strong subsequent effects on short-term investment and sales. If propagation effects matter because of changes in long-term capital, one might expect these effects on sales and short-term investments to be more gradual over time.
Another possibility is that shocks propagate over time by shaping firms’ internal resources and their ability to finance short-term investments, but without the leverage effects outlined in SectionI. For example, suppose that constrained firms borrow a constant dollar amount from suppliers (no leverage effect). Even in this case, negative shocks that hit in the main quarter could have stronger effects on their profits and net worth, forcing them to cut their subsequent short-term investments by more. This interpretation of our findings also implies funding constraints on short-term investments. While our main goal is not to distinguish between this interpretation and the mechanism outlined in SectionI, we note that our collective evidence is more consistent with the leverage effects outlined in SectionI. For example, we have direct evidence that firms that rely on supplier financing borrow more aggressively in their main quarter. Our story also explains why we find multiplier effects only for specific firms and shocks, and why changes in net worth have weaker effects on firms’ long-term investment.
As a final check to address these identification concerns, we examine whether these propagation effects are present for additional firms that are not exposed to our mechanism. As highlighted in SectionII, the mechanism we analyze should be present only when firms borrow more aggressively to fund their working capital in their main quarter, that is, when their leverage from suppliers increases in the main quarter. This pattern is a key necessary condition for the mechanism we analyze. We now examine whether the effects that we document remain relevant for additional firms where this pattern is not present. If propagation effects capture the mechanism we propose, we should not estimate significant effects in these samples. As we search for firms where this pattern is not empirically present, we focus on natural candidates for firms that are not exposed to significant liquidity issues.
We examine samples of large firms, high-cash firms, and high-payout firms. We construct these samples using firms in the top tercile in terms of their size (assets), payout ratio, or cash holdings in our initial database. Panel A of TableXshows that these firms do not borrow more aggressively to finance their working capital during their main quarter. These results replicate the analysis in Panel B of TableIIusing these alternative samples. In Panels B and C of TableX, we then examine the importance of propagation effects for sales and short-term investment in the response of these firms to shocks. Here, we replicate the estimates in Panel A of TablesIIIandIVusing these firms. The results show that the propagation effects we document are not present for any of these firms in the top tercile of size, payout, and cash. While it is difficult to completely rule out alternative interpretations for the propagation effects that we document, taken together our evidence is most consistent with the idea that the working capital credit multiplier drives these propagation effects.
article.VII. Conclusion:
In this paper, we provide new microlevel evidence that funding frictions lead an important subset of firms to limit short-term investments in payables and receivables, reducing their production capacity. We propose and analyze a new credit multiplier effect driven by these considerations (working capital credit multiplier) and show how these effects interact with seasonal patterns in firm profitability. These seasonal interactions are interesting in and of themselves because they show how initial conditions can shape credit multiplier effects. Seasonal interactions also help us empirically isolate the working capital credit multiplier. We detect the importance of this multiplier by comparing how responses of similar firms to recurring and unpredictable shocks differ when these shocks hit in the main quarter—the main quarter effect. We illustrate theoretically how the analysis of main quarter effects allows us to isolate the working capital credit multiplier and develop an identification strategy that considers predictions for these effects across different types of firms, investments (short- and long-term investments), and shocks. After implementing our empirical approach, we provide extensive evidence and discussion supporting our identification strategy.
Overall, our results suggest that these credit multiplier effect and funding constraints are significant for smaller U.S. firms that rely heavily on financing from suppliers (accounts payable), that is, top-supplier-financing firms. Across different definitions of smaller firms (outside the top size tercile to top size decile), these top-supplier-financing firms represent between 27% and 33% of the aggregate sales and assets of smaller firms in Compustat. These estimates can give an idea of how pervasive these effects are outside the very largest firms. In addition, it is plausible to imagine that these considerations are even more relevant for smaller private firms outside our sample.
Funding constraints on short-term investments have unique implications for the link between finance and the real economy, that contrast with the effects of financing frictions on long-term investment (primary focus of existing literature). At the macro level, the effect of short-term investments on production capacity can directly lower output through supply conditions. In contrast, changes in long-term investment affect aggregate output by changing aggregate demand conditions. This contrast can be important for explaining the aggregate effects of financial or economic shocks (e.g., Mendoza (2010), Jermann and Quadrini (2012), and Mendoza and Yue (2012)) as well as for understanding the effect of monetary policy shocks (Barth and Ramey (2001) and Christiano, Eichenbaum, and Evans (2005)). Connecting our microlevel evidence to these aggregate implications represents an interesting area for future research.
At the microlevel, shocks to firms’ funding conditions can have direct and immediate effects on their sales and operations, which contrast with delayed effects through changes in long-term capital. Moreover, as our analysis highlights, these funding constraints can lead to multiplier effects on liquidity as limits on firms’ production capacity induce further liquidity problems, and so on. This propagation effect illustrates the importance of incorporating financing frictions associated with short-term investments into analyses of firms’ financial and hedging policies.

We provide a simple model of the working capital credit multiplier that formalizes the intuitions discussed in the text.
Model Set Up
The model is a partial equilibrium model where some inputs are fixed (e.g., capital) and firms make choices of variable inputs over their cycle. As motivated in SectionI, firms cover operating costs prior to the collection of cash from sales and need working capital financing to fund this gap. Firms have to purchase the variable inputmt${m_t}$at the beginning of each periodtto generate (cash) revenueyt=Ai(φt)f(mt)=Ai(φt)mtβ${y_t} = {A_i}( {{\varphi _t}} )f( {{m_t}} ) = {A_i}( {{\varphi _t}} )m_t^\beta $at the end of that period, whereAi(φt)>0${A_i}( {{\varphi _t}} ) > 0$,0<β<1$0 < \beta < 1$,φt${\varphi _t}$captures demand conditions, andi$i$is a state reflecting seasonal operating conditions.27
Periods alternate between two statesi=H,L$i\ = \ H,L$, which can be interpreted as the two seasons of the year, andH$H$captures the firm's main period (higher profitability). The price of the variable input ispt${p_t}$. One interpretation for this timing is that firms sell on credit (receivables) at the start of each period and collect the cash from these sales at the end of the period. Another possibility is that firms buy inventories at the start of the period and sell the goods for cash at the end. These two possibilities are equivalent for our purposes. The key point is that, to generate revenue, the firm needs to finance a short-term investmentSIt≡ptmt$S{I_t} \equiv {p_t}{m_t}$at the start of the period. Suppliers provide short-term creditBt${B_t}$(working capital financing) at the beginning of each period and get paid at the end of the period when the firm generates cash from sales.28

This assumption avoids a scenario in which firms save in perpetuity and never pays dividends.
We examine a constrained firm that would like to expand its short-term investment using additional working capital financing but faces frictions. Response to Demand Shocks
Our main predictions hold for any shock, but the effects are stronger for shocks that have a stronger effect on firms’ input decisions, that is, when unlevered responses are more important. Firms’ unlevered responses to input price shocks should be more significant than responses to demand shocks because operating costs directly affect the upfront cost required form$m$. To show these points, we define firms’ responses to demand shocks (φ$\varphi $) in an analogous way to input price shocks. Model Extension: Long-Term Investment
In theInternet Appendix, we extend this simple framework to formally analyze the intuitions described in SectionIV.C. We show how these predictions follow from a few assumptions consistent with previous research and empirical evidence. As motivated in SectionIV.C, firms raise short-term (long-term) funding when expanding their short-term (long-term) investment.
